## I. THE SUBSTRATE ONTOLOGY

### I.A The Minimal Claim

The substrate is a unit-adjacency graph on Planck cells at Planck ticks, with direction-conditional continuous neighbors, remainder accumulation per tick, and channel-mediated adjacency extension. Nothing exists below this level.

The universe is not a continuous manifold. It is not a fabric. It is not a container holding matter and energy. It is a graph with arithmetic running on it. All observable structure — particles, atoms, molecules, planets, galaxies, the universal soliton — emerges from the graph's arithmetic.

### I.B The Five Primitives

**Cell:** A discrete spatial position at Planck scale. The smallest unit of space. No internal structure exists below this level. Cells are countable; the universe contains a finite number of them.

**Tick:** A discrete temporal advance at Planck time. The smallest unit of time. Between ticks, nothing happens. At each tick, substrate arithmetic updates the entire graph simultaneously.

**Direction-conditional adjacency:** Each cell has neighbors at exactly one Planck distance in any continuous direction. Direction is continuous; position is discrete; adjacency is direction-keyed. This is the topology that makes discrete substrate compatible with observed isotropy.

**Remainder:** Per-soliton accumulated vector quantity updated each tick. Decomposes into modulus (spherical sector) and remainder proper (two layers: number-theoretic and toroidal-geometric).

**Channel:** An adjacency edge between solitons that extends beyond cell-to-cell connection. Channels mediate remainder exchange, carry interaction effects across arbitrary graph distances, and can connect solitons whose Euclidean projections are arbitrarily far apart (entanglement case) while remaining graph-adjacent.

### I.C What the Substrate Does Per Tick

At each tick, the substrate runs one update cycle. The quiver contributes baseline activity at every cell. Channels compute their throughput for each soliton. Each soliton accumulates remainder from channels plus quiver extraction. Modulus crossings produce discrete state updates. Direction vectors update. Solitons advance one cell in their current direction vector (or cell-per-tick for massless patterns).

The update is uniform across the substrate. Every cell runs the same arithmetic. The complexity of observed phenomena emerges from compositions of the simple per-tick rule, not from elaborate per-location rules.

### I.D Minimality

No additional structure exists. The substrate does not have an external space in which it sits. There is no background stage. There is no container. The graph is what there is.

This minimality is load-bearing. Every observed phenomenon — mass, gravity, quantum mechanics, cosmological structure — is produced by the five primitives and their arithmetic. The framework commits that no new primitives will be needed. Whatever appears to require new primitives is either (a) a manifestation of existing primitives in unfamiliar configuration or (b) a misidentification of what the phenomenon is.

---

## II. CELLS AND TICKS

### II.A Cell as Position Primitive

Cells are the substrate's spatial primitive. Each cell is a structural point — a node in the substrate graph. Cells have no internal structure below this level. The question "what's inside a cell?" is not meaningful; cells are what space is made of.

Cells are countable. The universe contains a finite (very large) number of cells. This finiteness is what prevents the substrate from producing infinities in calculations. Every sum over cells is finite. Every integration that would diverge in a continuous manifold converges in the substrate because the graph has a bounded number of nodes.

Cells are not "points in space" in the continuous-manifold sense. They are positions defined by the adjacency graph. A cell's identity is its position in the graph, not coordinates on an underlying manifold.

### II.B Tick as Temporal Primitive

Ticks are the substrate's temporal primitive. Each tick is a discrete advance. Between ticks, nothing happens — there is no "during" a tick. At each tick boundary, the substrate's arithmetic runs atomically, updating all solitons and channels in one coordinated operation.

Ticks do not have duration in the classical sense; they are the unit of duration. The substrate's time is not a continuous parameter that ticks mark discretely. Time itself is the sequence of ticks.

### II.C Planck Scale as Local Configuration

The numerical values "Planck length = 1.616 × 10⁻³⁵ meters" and "Planck time = 5.391 × 10⁻⁴⁴ seconds" are Earth-local measurements. They are what the substrate's cell and tick durations are at our location in the universal soliton hierarchy.

Different locations in the hierarchy can have different local Planck values. A measurement of the Planck length at a significantly different substrate configuration — deep gravitational well, extreme density, remote cosmic epoch — would yield a different numerical value, reflecting the local substrate configuration rather than a universal constant.

What remains universal: the arithmetic identity c = 1 cell per tick. Wherever you measure, light advances one cell per tick, and the ratio of cells to ticks is 1. The absolute duration of a cell and the absolute duration of a tick may vary; their ratio does not.

### II.D The Universe Is Finite

The total count of cells in the universe is bounded. The total count of ticks since the universe's origin is bounded (finite cosmological age in finite-tick units). This boundedness is structural: the substrate has no way to produce an infinity because its native arithmetic is over finite counts.

This eliminates renormalization as a structural necessity. QFT's infinities are artifacts of assuming the substrate is a continuous manifold with integration over infinite momentum or infinite spatial extent. The actual substrate has neither. The observed need to subtract infinities in QFT is a computational consequence of computing in the continuum-limit approximation rather than in the substrate's native arithmetic.

---

## III. DIRECTION-CONDITIONAL ADJACENCY

### III.A The Graph Topology

Each cell has neighbors at exactly one Planck distance in any continuous direction. Direction is a continuous parameter (points on the unit sphere S²); distance is a discrete unit (1 Planck distance); adjacency is direction-keyed (which cell counts as "next" depends on the direction you specify).

This differs from a cubic lattice. In a cubic lattice, each cell has six axis-aligned neighbors; diagonal motion requires a staircase. The substrate has infinitely many neighbors (one in every direction), each at exactly unit distance. Diagonal motion is direct.

This differs from a hypergraph. The substrate is a graph (binary adjacency) with direction as the selection parameter, not a hypergraph (n-ary hyperedges).

### III.B Why This Produces Isotropy

The substrate has no preferred direction. Every direction on S² produces a neighbor at the same distance. Light traveling in any direction advances one cell per tick. No axis is privileged; no diagonal is slower than a cardinal.

Isotropy is structural in the substrate. Standard physics imposes isotropy as a property of the continuous manifold (the metric is Lorentz-invariant). The substrate produces it from the topology itself.

### III.C The Unit-Sphere Consequence

Because every neighbor is at exactly unit distance, the direction degrees of freedom at each cell form a unit sphere (S² in 3D space, S³ or higher in the mathematical generalization). Channel direction states live on this unit sphere by substrate construction — there are no non-unit directions because the substrate has no way to represent them.

This produces the Hilbert-sphere structure of quantum mechanics automatically. Standard QM postulates that states live on the unit sphere; the substrate delivers this without postulation. The unit sphere is the substrate's primitive adjacency structure, not an imposed constraint.

### III.D Graph, Not Manifold

The deepest structural commitment: the substrate is a graph, not a manifold.

A manifold has a continuous metric — any two points have a distance, any pair of points has a halfway point, you can always zoom in further. A graph has discrete nodes and edges — distances are integer counts of edges, there is no halfway, the graph cannot be refined.

Standard physics assumes a manifold. PCTRM commits to a graph. Every other consequence follows from this choice. Continuous coordinates are approximations; the substrate's native coordinates are graph positions and tick counts.

---

## IV. THE QUIVER — UNIVERSAL REMAINDER SOURCE

### IV.A The Substrate's Baseline Activity

The quiver is the substrate's per-tick arithmetic running continuously at every cell. At every tick, every cell has some activity — not zero. The activity is what the substrate is doing when no soliton is propagating through it.

This is the substrate being itself. The quiver is not a field imposed on the substrate; it is the substrate's own arithmetic operating at baseline. The distinction matters: fields in standard physics are additional mathematical objects defined on the manifold. The quiver is the substrate's intrinsic per-tick activity.

### IV.B What the Quiver Is Not

The quiver is not empty space. The concept of "empty space" at the substrate level does not exist — every cell has activity at every tick.

The quiver is not virtual particles. Standard QFT describes vacuum fluctuations as virtual particle pairs appearing and disappearing. The quiver is substrate activity; any particle-pair interpretation is a coarse-grained description of specific substrate configurations, not the substrate itself.

The quiver is not separate from the substrate. You cannot isolate the quiver from the substrate or remove it. It is the substrate's existence condition.

### IV.C Quiver as Universal Remainder Source

Every soliton extracts remainder from the quiver each tick. This is how solitons maintain themselves and advance. Without quiver extraction, solitons could not persist; their patterns would not have the per-tick remainder updates needed for continuation.

A photon extracts one cell's worth of direction-vector remainder per tick — this is exactly what's needed for one-cell-per-tick advance.

A massive soliton extracts remainder from the quiver, then pays Higgs tick-cost, then applies gravitational drain, then updates its direction. The net result is less than one cell per tick of forward motion because the Higgs tax has consumed part of the quiver extraction.

A composite soliton (atom, molecule, planet) extracts from the quiver at every constituent position, runs internal channel dynamics, engages gravitational hierarchy, and produces the net observable motion that emerges from the sum.

### IV.D Connection to Standard Vacuum Physics

The Casimir effect is the quiver's channel activity differences between bounded and unbounded regions. Standard physics calculates this via vacuum fluctuations; the substrate picture calculates it via quiver channel structure between close surfaces.

The Lamb shift is the electron's quiver coupling modified by its bound-state configuration. Atomic bound states have different channel structures than free electrons; the quiver extraction through those structures differs, producing the measurable shift.

The cosmological constant is the universal-scale partition of quiver activity at the universal soliton's boundary. The specific value (251 - 22π)/264 is the substrate's integer alphabet expression of this universal-scale quiver contribution.

---

## V. SOLITONS AND THE HIERARCHY

### V.A What a Soliton Is

A soliton is a stable, self-sustaining pattern in substrate arithmetic. The substrate's configuration at specific cells maintains a characteristic structure across ticks — the pattern persists even as individual cell-level values update.

Solitons are not substances. They are patterns. The cells "in" a soliton are not special; they participate in the soliton's pattern at the current tick but can participate in different patterns at different ticks. What is stable is the pattern itself — the configuration of channels, modulus values, and remainder accumulation that defines the soliton's identity.

Mass is inertia — resistance to pattern change. Substance is not in this equation. What standard physics calls a particle is a soliton pattern sustaining itself through the substrate's quiver extraction and channel dynamics.

### V.B The Pattern-Not-Substance Commitment

An electron is not a small solid ball. It is a specific pattern in substrate arithmetic — a configuration of channels and modulus values at a specific location that maintains itself tick after tick. The cells participating in the electron's pattern at tick T may participate in different patterns at tick T+1 as the electron advances in direction; the electron's identity is the pattern, not the cells.

A proton is a composite pattern — a soliton containing three quark solitons, gluon channel patterns, and a containing boundary. Its 99% "pattern-binding-energy" component and 1% "quark content" component both reduce to Higgs-tick-cost at different substrate layers. The distinction between "constituent mass" and "binding mass" is a Euclidean-coordinate artifact; at the substrate level, all mass is Higgs-induced tick-cost for pattern maintenance.

A galaxy is a vast composite pattern — a soliton containing stars (which are themselves composite solitons containing atoms) plus toroidal flow structures plus dark-matter-classification substrate dynamics. The galaxy's identity is the pattern, which persists even as individual stars orbit, form, and extinguish within it.

### V.C The Seven-Level Hierarchy

The substrate organizes patterns into a nested hierarchy of seven characteristic levels:

**Level 0 — Substrate:** Planck cells and ticks. The adjacency graph itself. Characteristic scale ~10⁻³⁵ m. Examples: individual cells, single-tick channel activations, the quiver.

**Level 1 — Subatomic:** Elementary particles. Electrons, quarks, neutrinos, photons, gluons, W/Z bosons, Higgs. Characteristic scale ~10⁻¹⁸ m. Solitons at this level are fundamental patterns with no internal soliton structure at Level 1.

**Level 2 — Atomic:** Atoms. Hydrogen through heavy elements. Characteristic scale ~10⁻¹⁰ m. Solitons at this level contain nuclear soliton (Level 3) plus electron solitons (Level 1) in bound configurations.

**Level 3 — Nuclear:** Nucleons and nuclei. Protons, neutrons, deuterons, helium nuclei, up to heavy nuclei. Characteristic scale ~10⁻¹⁵ m. Solitons at this level contain quark solitons in confinement-channel patterns.

**Level 4 — Molecular:** Molecules. From diatomic to macromolecular. Characteristic scale ~10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁵ m. Solitons contain atomic solitons in bound configurations.

**Level 5 — Macroscopic:** Planets, stars, stellar systems. Characteristic scale 10⁶ to 10¹² m. Solitons contain all smaller hierarchical levels as nested structure.

**Level 6 — Cosmological:** Galaxies, galaxy clusters, universal soliton. Characteristic scale 10²¹ m and up. The universal soliton is the outermost boundary of the hierarchy.

### V.D Interface and Implementation

The same vocabulary applies at every hierarchy level. Every level has solitons, channels, remainder, modulus. This is the interface universality.

What differs by level is the implementation: per-hierarchy-boundary modulus values, channel-type distribution, characteristic time scales. A proton's channel structure differs from a galaxy's channel structure, but both are channels with the same substrate-level definition.

This interface-universality is what makes cross-scale predictions possible. The same integer alphabet produces particle-physics and cosmological parameters because both emerge from the same substrate vocabulary applied at different levels.

### V.E Parent-Child Structure

Every soliton below the universal level has a parent soliton — the next higher level that contains it. Every soliton above Level 0 has child solitons — the next lower level that it contains.

The Earth is the Sun's child in Level 5. The Moon is the Earth's child in Level 5. The Moon is not directly the Sun's child; it is the Sun's grandchild, with Earth as the intermediate parent.

Sibling solitons share a parent. The Moon and Earth's artificial satellites are siblings (all are Earth's children). Stars in the Milky Way are siblings (all are the galaxy's children).

### V.F The Universal Soliton

The universal soliton is Level 6's boundary. It is not a "universe" in a container; it is itself a soliton with internal structure. Its internal structure is the nested hierarchy of all smaller solitons.

The universal soliton has its own modulus, its own remainder, its own channel structure. Its modulus and remainder produce the cosmological partition (Ω_DM = π/12, Ω_b = 13/264, Ω_Λ = (251 - 22π)/264). Its boundary defines what's "inside" the observable universe.

What exists outside the universal soliton is outside the scope of PCTRM. The framework does not extend to other universal solitons or to containing structures above Level 6. The universal soliton is the hierarchy's top.

---

## VI. CHANNELS

### VI.A Channel as Adjacency Extension

A channel is an adjacency edge between solitons that extends beyond cell-to-cell direct adjacency. While cells are adjacent to neighbors at one Planck distance, channels connect solitons that may be at substantial Euclidean separation — or, in the entanglement case, at arbitrary Euclidean separation.

Channels are soliton-bound, not cell-bound. As a soliton advances through cells, its channels move with it. An electron with an EM channel to a proton maintains that channel as it moves through cells; the channel's endpoint is the electron (the soliton), not a specific cell.

### VI.B Channel Properties

Each channel has:

- **Endpoints:** Two or more solitons. For most channels, exactly two endpoints (binary). For entanglement channels involving multi-particle states (GHZ, W, cluster), more endpoints possible.
- **Direction:** A vector in each endpoint's frame specifying the channel's spatial orientation.
- **Throughput:** The remainder exchanged per tick across the channel.
- **Activation state:** Always-active or conditionally-active. Always-active channels (gravity, EM, strong, thermal, Higgs) operate continuously. Conditionally-active channels (entanglement, weak, specific interaction-triggered) activate and deactivate based on specific substrate-level conditions.
- **Sector content:** Spherical sector contribution and toroidal sector contribution. Each channel may carry both sectors in different proportions depending on its type.

### VI.C Channel Type Catalog

**Gravitational (drain):** Always active. Direction: radially inward toward parent soliton's center. Throughput: proportional to parent mass (parent's Higgs-tick-cost aggregate) divided by r² (spherical channel spreading). Sectors: spherical dominant at low probe resolution, toroidal contributing at high probe resolution. Scale: all hierarchy levels. Produces 1/r² gravity plus GR corrections.

**Electromagnetic:** Always active between electrically charged solitons. Direction: continuous vector in each endpoint's frame. Throughput: proportional to q₁q₂/r². Sectors: mostly spherical with toroidal content for magnetic effects. Scale: Levels 0-6. Produces Coulomb interaction plus magnetic and radiative effects.

**Strong (confinement):** Always active within nucleon boundary. Direction: toroidal flux tube. Throughput: extremely high, confined within Level 3 soliton boundary. Sectors: primarily toroidal (flux tubes). Scale: Level 3. Produces quark confinement and 99% of nucleon mass.

**Strong (residual):** Conditionally active. Triggered when nucleons are within binding range. Direction: between nucleon centers. Throughput: moderate, shorter range than EM. Sectors: toroidal. Scale: Level 3. Produces nuclear binding.

**Weak:** Conditionally active. Triggered by specific flavor-changing or parity-violating interaction events. Direction: interaction-specific. Throughput: small compared to strong/EM. Sectors: mixed. Scale: Levels 1-3. Produces weak decays, neutrino oscillation, CP violation.

**Thermal:** Always active. Direction: omnidirectional, random at substrate level but producing coherent thermal effects at higher hierarchy levels. Throughput: temperature-dependent. Sectors: primarily spherical. Scale: all levels. Contributes to decoherence competition.

**Entanglement:** Conditionally active. Triggered by specific interaction events (decay into correlated final state, scattering producing entangled output, engineered preparation). Direction: channel vector in each endpoint's frame. Throughput: carries correlation, not energy. Sectors: both spherical (for correlation magnitude) and toroidal (for phase content). Endpoints can be at arbitrary Euclidean separation. Scale: all levels where QM is operative. Produces Bell correlations, GHZ correlations, quantum entanglement phenomena.

**Higgs:** Always active for massive solitons. Direction: not spatial — adds ticks-per-cycle cost rather than directional throughput. Throughput: mass-dependent, modulates the cell-per-tick advance rate of the affected soliton. Sectors: modulates both spherical and toroidal contributions. Scale: Levels 1-6 for massive matter. Produces inertial mass, gravitational mass, and the mass hierarchy.

**Channel-sharing (merger):** Conditionally active during entanglement and observation events. Pattern-global rather than endpoint-to-endpoint. When channel-sharing is active, what appeared as separate solitons are revealed as one pattern with multiple Euclidean handles. Sectors: both. Scale: all levels. Is the mechanism underlying entanglement, observation-as-entanglement, and measurement-without-collapse.

### VI.D Channel Composition

Multiple channels can act on the same soliton simultaneously. A child soliton experiences gravity from its parent, EM from nearby charged solitons, thermal noise from its environment, and potentially entanglement channels to specific other solitons. Each tick, all active channels contribute to the soliton's remainder accumulation.

The composition rule: contributions are vector-summed. Each channel provides a direction-vector contribution per tick; the soliton's next-tick direction update is the sum of all channel contributions plus quiver extraction, minus Higgs tick-cost.

Channel composition is not approximate. It is the substrate's native arithmetic. The soliton's observable motion is the result of this composition, not a derived approximation.

### VI.E Channel Soliton-Binding

Channels are bound to solitons, not to cells. This is a specific structural commitment. As solitons move, their channels move with them. As solitons merge, their channels participate in the merger. As solitons dissolve (lose pattern coherence), their channels terminate.

This soliton-binding is what makes entanglement persist over arbitrary distances. The entangled particles' channel is attached to each particle. As the particles separate in space, the channel is stretched in the Euclidean projection but remains a single graph edge at the substrate level.

## VII. DUAL-GEOMETRY SECTORS

### VII.A Every Soliton Has Two Geometric Sectors

Every soliton in the hierarchy has two geometric components: a spherical sector (called the modulus) and a toroidal sector (called the remainder). This is a universal structural commitment.

The spherical sector comprises angular operations on spherical subspaces of the channel structure. It produces contributions with β = π/4 as the conversion factor (MATH-11). Exponent counting: β² counts one angular integration on a sphere, β⁴ counts two, β⁶ counts three.

The toroidal sector comprises angular operations on topological tori within the channel structure. It produces contributions with K(k)/π as the conversion factor (MATH-12) where k is the topology-specific modulus. Exponent counting: K(k)² counts one angular integration on a torus, K(k)³ counts two, K(k) × E(k) counts a mixed cycle.

### VII.B The Three-Layer Decomposition

Every soliton's remainder decomposes into three layers:

**Spherical modulus (β²+ content):** Angular operations on spherical subspaces. Always present. Scales with β², β⁴, β⁶ etc. depending on the operation's depth.

**Number-theoretic β⁰ (Layer 1):** Radial integrations, diagram combinatorics, polylogarithms. Rational numbers, ζ values, Li values. Always present at all computational depths. Has no π content directly.

**Toroidal-geometric β⁰ (Layer 2):** Angular operations on topological tori. K(k), E(k) and their products at topology-specific moduli. First appearance in QED at four-loop level via topologies 81 and 83. Has no π content in the raw integral but contains π through elliptic-form products (K×π, E×π, K²/π).

### VII.C Dual-Geometry at Every Hierarchy Level

The dual-geometry structure manifests at every level of the soliton hierarchy:

**Level 3 (Nuclear):** The proton has a roughly spherical confinement boundary (modulus) containing toroidal gluon flux tubes (remainder). The flux tubes carry 99% of the proton's mass.

**Level 2 (Atomic):** The atom has roughly spherical electron shells (modulus) and a toroidal magnetic moment structure (remainder).

**Level 5 (Planetary):** The Earth has roughly spherical atmospheric layers (modulus) and the toroidal Van Allen radiation belts (remainder).

**Level 6 (Cosmological):** The galaxy has a roughly spherical dark-matter halo (modulus) and a toroidal disk (remainder). The universal soliton has its own spherical and toroidal sectors producing the cosmic partition.

**Level 0 (QED four-loop):** Quantum electrodynamics at four-loop precision has spherical π terms (modulus) and toroidal Laporta constants (remainder), validated at sub-ppm precision with extracted moduli k₈₁ = 0.999994 and k₈₃ = 0.997130.

### VII.D Probe-Dependent Sector Dominance

Different probes resolve different sectors. The general principle: the probe's ability to resolve toroidal structure scales with (probe_wavelength⁻¹ / source_toroidal_scale⁻¹)². Long-wavelength probes see primarily the spherical sector; short-wavelength probes see the toroidal sector.

At QED four-loop precision: the electron (Compton wavelength ~10⁻¹² m) probes primarily the spherical sector; the muon (Compton wavelength ~10⁻¹⁴ m, 207× shorter) probes primarily the toroidal sector with 2304% amplification over the universal spherical contribution.

The crossover is at approximately 43 m_e ≈ 22 MeV. Below this mass, spherical dominance; above, toroidal dominance.

The same principle applies at gravitational scale. Visible light bending near the Sun probes the Sun's spherical sector (Newtonian 1/r²) plus toroidal sector at photon wavelength (the factor-of-2 correction in GR). Heavier probes of gravitational fields (gravitational wave detectors sensitive to smaller-scale structure) would probe finer toroidal content.

### VII.E Topology-Specific Moduli

Each topological structure has its own modulus k. The framework has established two at sub-ppm cross-derivation precision:

**k₈₁ = 0.999994** (spread 167 ppb across three independent derivation paths), characteristic of Laporta topology 81 in QED four-loop computation.

**k₈₃ = 0.997130** (spread 25 ppm across three independent derivation paths), characteristic of Laporta topology 83 in QED four-loop computation.

Additional moduli per hierarchy boundary are pending derivation. Each soliton hierarchy boundary has its own characteristic modulus, derivable from the integer alphabet expressions specific to the boundary's structural properties.

## VIII. THE HIERARCHICAL COORDINATE SYSTEM

### VIII.A Running Reading Depth as Primary Coordinate

Position in the substrate is defined hierarchically. There is no universal Cartesian grid. A soliton's position is specified by:

1. Which parent soliton it is within
2. Its position within that parent (running reading depth — how deep into the parent's structure)
3. Its relative position to sibling solitons within the same parent

This recursive definition proceeds up the hierarchy to the universal soliton.

The Earth's position is specified as: "within the Sun's hierarchy, at radial depth 1 AU (Sun-local Planck cells), at specific orbital phase relative to sibling planets." The Moon's position is: "within Earth's hierarchy, at radial depth 384,000 km (Earth-local Planck cells), at specific orbital phase."

### VIII.B No Universal Grid

The universe is not a single Cartesian coordinate system. It is a hierarchy of nested coordinate systems, one per soliton level. Each soliton's internal structure defines its children's coordinate frame.

Attempts to unify everything into a single Cartesian grid (heliocentric coordinates for solar-system navigation, cosmic microwave background frame for cosmological measurements) are projections of the hierarchical system into a convenient single-coordinate approximation. The projections work for practical purposes because the hierarchical structure is smooth at macroscopic scales, but the underlying substrate is hierarchical.

### VIII.C Planck-Cell Counts are Hierarchy-Local

The number of Planck cells between Earth and Moon is defined within Earth's hierarchy — it is counted in Earth-local Planck cells. The number of Planck cells between Sun and Earth is defined within Sun's hierarchy — Sun-local Planck cells.

These counts are not directly comparable across hierarchies. Asking "how many Planck cells are between the Sun and the Moon?" is a category error; the question assumes a universal coordinate system that does not exist in the substrate.

What is meaningfully asked: "How many Planck cells are between Sun and Earth in Sun's hierarchy, plus how many between Earth and Moon in Earth's hierarchy?" These are two separate quantities in two separate coordinate systems that happen to relate through the hierarchy boundary at Earth's Hill sphere.

### VIII.D Hierarchy Boundaries

Soliton hierarchies transition at specific substrate-level boundaries:

**Hill sphere:** The boundary between a parent's hierarchy and its grandparent's. Earth's Hill sphere is approximately 1.5 million km. Inside, cells are counted in Earth's hierarchy. Outside, in the Sun's.

**Galactic halo boundary:** The boundary between galactic solitons at Level 6.

**Nucleon boundary:** The boundary between Level 3 nuclear solitons and the quarks they contain.

At each boundary, the coordinate system shifts. Crossing the boundary is not a smooth continuous transition in the substrate's native structure; it's a shift between two different coordinate systems.

Observationally, the boundaries appear smooth because the substrate's hierarchical transitions produce continuous-looking behavior when projected into Euclidean coordinates. At high precision, the transitions may produce measurable discontinuities — not in the observable phenomena themselves, but in how calculations using different coordinate systems compose.

### VIII.E Motion in Hierarchical Coordinates

Motion is hierarchical. A soliton's velocity is defined relative to its parent soliton's frame. The velocity contributions compose hierarchically when translating to other reference frames.

A star in the Milky Way's disk has two motion components: its motion relative to the galaxy's toroidal flow frame (its peculiar velocity), and the galactic toroid's rotation through the universal soliton. The measured velocity as seen from another galaxy is the composition of these.

This produces the resolution of the "rim stars moving faster than light" puzzle. Rim stars have small peculiar velocity within the toroidal flow frame — they are essentially co-moving with the flow. The apparent high velocity seen from outside the galaxy is the toroid's rotation, not the star's individual motion through the substrate. No soliton moves through the substrate faster than c; the toroid's rotation is a coherent substrate configuration whose rotation does not require individual components to exceed c.

### VIII.F Coordinate Transformation Across Hierarchies

When observation requires crossing hierarchy levels (observer and observed are in different hierarchies or at different levels), coordinate transformation through the hierarchy is required.

The transformation preserves the integer alphabet relationships (dimensionless ratios are invariant) and the structural topology (graph adjacency patterns). It does not preserve absolute dimensional quantities (Planck-cell counts are hierarchy-local).

Euclidean approximations to the coordinate transformation work at macroscopic scales because the hierarchical structure is smooth and the scale differences between hierarchy levels are large. At high precision, the approximations may show systematic residuals that correspond to the hierarchy structure.

## IX. PLANCK-SCALE LOCALITY

### IX.A Planck Values are Measurements, Not Constants

The Planck length (approximately 10⁻³⁵ m) and Planck time (approximately 10⁻⁴⁴ s) are measured quantities. They are what the substrate's cell and tick durations are at the specific configuration where they are measured.

Measured values derive from the Earth-local substrate configuration — Earth's position within the Sun's hierarchy within the Milky Way's hierarchy within the universal soliton. A measurement performed at a substantially different substrate configuration (deep gravitational well, different cosmic epoch, different galactic location) could yield measurably different values.

### IX.B What Varies and What Does Not

Invariant across hierarchies:
- c = 1 cell per tick (substrate arithmetic identity)
- The integer alphabet (13, 22, 264, 251, etc.)
- Dimensionless ratios produced by the alphabet (π/12, 13/264, 22π/13, etc.)
- Structural topology (graph adjacency, direction-conditional rule)
- Channel type catalog
- The three-layer decomposition (modulus + number-theoretic + toroidal)

Variant across hierarchies:
- Planck length in SI units
- Planck time in SI units
- Coupling constants at specific scales
- Absolute dimensional measurements
- "Fundamental constants" as measured quantities

### IX.C The H₀ Tension as Hierarchy Configuration Difference

The SH0ES vs Planck H₀ measurement disagreement (about 8-9%) arises from the two measurements sampling different hierarchy configurations:

SH0ES measures H₀ using a local distance ladder (supernovae in relatively nearby galaxies), sampling our galactic neighborhood's substrate configuration.

Planck measures H₀ from the cosmic microwave background, sampling the universe at the time of last scattering — a different cosmic configuration with different local substrate properties.

The framework predicts the ratio between these measurements as 12/11, arising from the transit-count interpretation (12 boundary crossings for local-measurement configurations, 11 for CMB-derived configurations). This is a structural prediction, not a new physics proposal.

### IX.D Gravitational Redshift as Tick Rate Measurement

Gravitational redshift — light emitted from a lower gravitational potential appears redshifted to an observer at higher potential — is a direct measurement of local tick rate differences.

At lower gravitational potential, the substrate's local tick duration is longer. Light emitted there has lower frequency (fewer oscillations per observer's tick) as seen from higher potential.

This is the substrate-level mechanism underlying gravitational time dilation. Standard GR describes it as spacetime curvature; the substrate describes it as hierarchy-dependent tick rate variation.

### IX.E Coupling Constants Run with Hierarchy

Standard physics accepts that coupling constants run with energy scale. The substrate picture adds: they also run with hierarchy configuration.

G, α, α_s, and other "coupling constants" are measurements of substrate configuration effects. At different hierarchies (different substrate configurations), they have slightly different values. The running with energy scale is one instance of this general principle; the running with hierarchy position is another.

The framework's predictions for coupling values at specific measurements should account for both the energy scale and the hierarchy configuration of the measurement.

## X. PHOTON PROPAGATION AND c-INVARIANCE

### X.A What a Photon Is

A photon is a pattern-of-coupling to the substrate's quiver. It extracts one cell's worth of direction-vector remainder per tick and advances one cell in its direction.

The photon has no Higgs interaction. It carries no mass. Its pattern is sustained purely by quiver extraction and direction propagation; no internal tick-cost from Higgs coupling.

The photon's frequency corresponds to the spatial period of its pattern-of-coupling. Different frequencies are different spatial periods of the same basic pattern structure.

### X.B Cell-per-Tick as c

The speed of light c = 1 cell per tick. This is substrate arithmetic, not postulation.

Between a photon's emission at cell A and absorption at cell B, there are exactly N cells. The photon takes exactly N ticks to traverse them. N/N = 1 cell per tick. Any observer who counts cells and ticks will get 1 cell per tick regardless of how N was chosen.

This is the substrate's definition of c. The numerical value 299,792,458 m/s is the conversion into SI units, which themselves are Earth-local measurements of the substrate's local cell and tick durations.

### X.C c-Invariance for Photons

c-invariance for photons is an arithmetic identity. The photon takes N ticks to traverse N cells; this ratio is 1 regardless of the specific N chosen.

This handles the photon piece of Lorentz invariance. Observers in different inertial frames will each measure c as 1 cell per tick in their local frames because each observer's measurement uses their local cells and their local ticks, and the ratio is invariant.

Full Lorentz invariance requires the observer piece — how observers' clocks and rulers transform under boosts. This is specified in Section XI below as relating to the Higgs tick-cost structure for massive observers.

### X.D Observers as Absorbers

Observers do not watch photons in flight. Photons are unobservable except at their endpoints — emission and absorption.

An observer registers emission events (photon originates from a source interaction) and absorption events (photon terminates at the observer's sensor). Between these two events, no observer can "see" the photon. Attempting to do so would be an absorption event, which ends the photon.

This is a structural property of how observation works in the substrate. Observation is always an interaction. Interactions are always channel events. There is no passive watching. What we call "measuring the speed of light" involves registering emission and absorption and computing distance/time between them.

### X.E Gravitational Light Bending

A photon passing through a gravitational field does not slow down. Its cell-per-tick advance rate remains unchanged. What changes is the photon's direction vector.

The gravitational channel from a parent soliton applies direction-biased remainder to the photon each tick. The bias is directed toward the parent soliton's center. The photon's quiver extraction continues at one cell per tick, but the direction of that cell advance rotates each tick by the amount the gravitational channel contributes.

Accumulated over the photon's transit through the gravitational field, this produces light bending. The bending angle is the sum of direction-rotation contributions over the photon's transit.

The Newtonian bending angle comes from the spherical sector of the gravitational channel. The factor-of-2 correction in GR comes from the toroidal sector becoming visible at photon wavelength scale near the source. The total bending is the sum: spherical contribution + toroidal contribution, with the toroidal contribution scaling with the inverse of the photon wavelength relative to the source's toroidal channel scale.

### X.F Photon Frequency and Substrate Structure

Photon frequency corresponds to the spatial wavelength of the pattern-of-coupling. Different frequencies are different spatial periods.

At very high frequencies (short wavelengths approaching the Planck scale), only specific wavelengths fit the substrate's cell structure cleanly. This produces a structural UV cutoff at the Planck scale — no photons with wavelength shorter than one Planck distance can exist as coherent patterns.

This is an automatic consequence of the substrate's discreteness, not a postulated cutoff. Standard QFT introduces UV cutoffs ad hoc for calculational convenience; the substrate's cutoff emerges from the cell structure.

## XI. MASS, INERTIA, AND THE HIGGS MECHANISM

### XI.A Mass as Pattern, Not Substance

Mass is inertia — resistance to pattern change. It is not a substance contained in the particle; it is a measure of how much pattern-reconfiguration cost the soliton pays per direction update.

The operational equation: m = F / a. Applied force F produces acceleration a. The mass m is the resistance parameter. In the substrate picture, F is the drain-bias applied per tick (force is remainder direction-bias) and a is the resulting direction change per tick. The resistance m is the Higgs pattern reconfiguration cost per direction update.

Substance is not in this equation. It has no place in the substrate picture.

### XI.B Higgs Interactions as Tick-Cost

A massive soliton couples to the Higgs pattern. This coupling produces a per-tick cost: before the soliton's direction vector can update, the Higgs pattern must be reconfigured to match the new direction.

The Higgs reconfiguration costs remainder. Each tick, the soliton's quiver extraction must pay this cost before any remainder is available for direction advance. The net result: the soliton advances fewer cells per tick than a massless particle would.

Different particles have different Higgs coupling strengths. Stronger Higgs coupling means more reconfiguration cost per tick, which means slower effective propagation. This produces the observed mass hierarchy — different particles have different inertial masses because they have different Higgs coupling strengths.

### XI.C Mass Hierarchy from Per-Boundary Modulus

Mass values across the particle spectrum are produced by the per-hierarchy-boundary modulus structure (Q10 from the Q-question catalog). Each hierarchy boundary has its own modulus, and the masses of particles at different hierarchy levels are derivable from these moduli through the integer alphabet.

The electron mass, muon mass, tau mass, up/down/strange/charm/bottom/top quark masses — all are expressions in the integer alphabet with β and K(k) at appropriate moduli. The ratios between them (m_μ/m_e, m_τ/m_e, etc.) are alphabet-derivable.

This is the Higgs-coupling-to-per-tick-budget formula: given a particle's hierarchy position and interaction structure, its Higgs coupling and therefore its mass is determined by the substrate's integer alphabet expression for its boundary.

### XI.D Why Photons Are Massless

Photons have no Higgs coupling. They do not participate in the Higgs pattern's tick-cost mechanism. Each tick, a photon's quiver extraction directly becomes direction advance — no reconfiguration cost to pay.

This is why photons propagate at c (one cell per tick) while massive particles propagate at less than c. Not because photons are "exempt" from some tax, but because the Higgs coupling mechanism simply does not apply to them.

Similarly, gluons in the massless approximation and the various neutrino mass eigenstates (very weakly coupled to Higgs) show this structure to different degrees.

### XI.E Gravitational Mass Equals Inertial Mass

The gravitational mass (the drain-source magnitude a soliton produces on its children) equals the inertial mass (the reconfiguration cost the soliton pays per direction update). Both reduce to the aggregate Higgs-tick-cost of the soliton's internal pattern structure.

This is the equivalence principle at substrate level. It is not coincidence; it is because both quantities are measurements of the same underlying substrate property — the soliton's Higgs pattern aggregation.

### XI.F The Proton Mass Breakdown Resolved

Standard physics says 99% of the proton's mass comes from "binding energy" (gluon field energy) and 1% from "quark content" (up and down quark masses). This has always felt like a strange discrepancy — most of the mass isn't the matter itself.

In the substrate picture, this is not a discrepancy. All mass is Higgs-tick-cost for pattern maintenance. The proton's pattern has multiple layers: the quark solitons (each with their own Higgs coupling), the gluon channel patterns binding them (each with their own pattern maintenance requiring Higgs-coupling for massive gluon modes at strong coupling), and the confinement boundary structure (requiring pattern maintenance).

The "99% binding energy" is the Higgs-tick-cost of the gluon channel patterns and confinement boundary. The "1% quark content" is the Higgs-tick-cost of the individual quark solitons. Both are the same kind of quantity — Higgs-induced tick-cost at different substrate layers — and there is no mystery about why one is much larger than the other. The gluon channel patterns are simply more extensive than the individual quark patterns.

### XI.G Observer Tick-Rate Scaling and Time Dilation

Observers moving at different velocities have different tick rates relative to each other because their Higgs coupling structures process at different rates in their relative frames.

A massive observer at rest in their local frame has some Higgs tick-cost per cycle (determining their clock rate). A massive observer moving at high velocity in another frame has their Higgs pattern being processed through additional direction-change cycles (the motion itself requires continuous Higgs reconfiguration), which changes the effective tick rate of their clock.

This produces time dilation. An observer moving at high velocity runs their clock slower (more ticks consumed by motion-related Higgs reconfiguration, fewer ticks available for clock oscillations).

The photon's c-invariance is preserved because photons don't have Higgs coupling; their cell-per-tick advance is not affected by observer motion. Massive observers' clocks slow with motion, so they measure photons taking the expected proper time across their local cells.

Full Lorentz invariance emerges from this: photons always travel at c in any frame (arithmetic identity), and massive observers' clocks transform exactly such that their measurement of c in their own frame remains c. The transformation is the hyperbolic time dilation formula of special relativity, emerging from the substrate's Higgs-tick-cost structure for moving observers.

## XII. GRAVITATIONAL DYNAMICS

### XII.A Gravity as Channel Drain

Gravity is the drain channel from a parent soliton to its children. The parent soliton applies negative remainder to children in a direction radially inward toward its center. This drain is what gravitational attraction is at the substrate level.

The drain magnitude is proportional to the parent's mass (aggregate Higgs-tick-cost) divided by r² where r is the child's radial distance from the parent's center (in the parent's hierarchy's coordinate system). This 1/r² scaling comes from spherical channel spreading — the drain channel spreads over a sphere, and its density falls as 1/surface_area = 1/r².

The drain direction at each child's location is the direction of the vector from the child's position to the parent's center. The drain's contribution to the child's per-tick remainder update is this radial inward direction.

### XII.B Parent-Child Tax Hierarchy

Gravitational dynamics operates hierarchically. Each child soliton experiences only its immediate parent's drain, not its grandparent's or higher levels' drain directly.

The Moon experiences Earth's gravitational drain (Moon is Earth's child). The Moon does not directly experience the Sun's drain (the Moon is not the Sun's child — the Sun's child is Earth, and the Moon is Earth's child in its own hierarchy).

What standard physics calls "tidal effects from the Sun on the Moon" are mediated by the Sun's drain on the Earth. Earth's motion through the Sun's drain creates a differential drain environment that the Moon experiences as part of being in Earth's hierarchy. Same measurable effect, substrate-level hierarchical mechanism.

### XII.C Sibling Interactions

Sibling solitons at the same hierarchy level interact through the channels appropriate to their interaction types (EM, thermal, etc.). Sibling gravitational interactions are minor compared to parent-child drain and are often negligible at macroscopic scales.

Solar-system dynamics: Earth and Jupiter are siblings (both are Sun's children). Their direct gravitational effect on each other is small compared to the Sun's effect on each. Standard N-body calculations include these sibling effects as perturbations; the substrate picture describes them the same way but at smaller magnitude than parent-child effects.

### XII.D The Non-N-Body Commitment

The N-body problem as standard physics formulates it — summing gravitational attractions from all masses in the universe on all other masses — is a Euclidean-coordinate approximation, not a substrate-level reality.

The substrate does not sum attractions across arbitrary hierarchy levels. Each soliton experiences its immediate parent's drain. The complexity of observed dynamics (planetary orbits, sibling perturbations, multi-body problems) emerges from the hierarchical composition of parent-child relations, not from an N-body sum.

Practically, standard N-body calculations produce correct observations because they approximate the hierarchical structure sufficiently well at solar-system scales. At higher precision, hierarchical calculations should produce slightly different results at hierarchy boundary crossings.

### XII.E 1/r² from Spherical Channel Spreading

The Newtonian 1/r² gravitational law comes from the drain channel spreading over a sphere. Channel density at radius r is proportional to 1/(4πr²). Gravitational force per unit mass equals this channel density. Therefore gravitational acceleration scales as 1/r².

This is the spherical sector of gravity — the modulus of the gravitational channel. It produces all of Newtonian gravity from substrate structure.

### XII.F GR Corrections from Toroidal Content

The factor-of-2 correction to light bending (GR over Newton), Mercury's perihelion precession (43″/century), Shapiro delay (light-travel-time variations near massive bodies), and other General Relativity corrections emerge from the toroidal sector of the gravitational channel.

The toroidal content becomes visible to probes at characteristic scales. Photons at optical wavelengths near the Sun resolve the Sun's toroidal channel structure at photon wavelength scale, producing the factor-of-2 correction. Mercury's orbit resolves the Sun's toroidal structure at Mercury's orbital scale, producing precession. Different probes at different scales resolve different fractions of the toroidal content.

The scaling follows (probe_scale⁻¹ / source_scale⁻¹)² — analogous to the (m_probe/m_e)² scaling observed at QED in PHYS-49.

### XII.G Galactic Rotation as Toroidal Flow

Galactic rotation curves — flat at the galactic rim when Newtonian gravity from visible mass predicts declining velocities — are explained by the galaxy's toroidal flow as a substrate configuration.

The galaxy as parent soliton has a spherical halo sector (visible baryonic center plus geometric extent) and a toroidal disk sector. Stars in the disk are embedded in the toroidal flow — they are carried by the flow, not moving independently through space.

What observers measure as "rim star rotational velocity" is primarily the galactic toroidal flow's rotation as a coherent substrate configuration. Individual rim stars have small peculiar velocities relative to the flow frame; these are essentially invisible at the resolution of standard rotation curve measurements.

The 22π/13 DM/baryon ratio emerges from this: the toroidal flow's Higgs response (how the flow affects the Higgs pattern of objects within it) is what produces the gravitational effect standard astrophysics attributes to "dark matter particles." There are no dark matter particles in this picture; there is only toroidal flow dynamics producing Higgs response.

### XII.H No Dark Matter Particles

The framework commits: there are no dark matter particles. The observed gravitational effects attributed to dark matter are produced by toroidal flow dynamics at galactic and cosmic scales. What standard astrophysics interprets as "missing mass" is a Euclidean-coordinate misattribution of flow-carrying kinematics to individual-star motion.

This is a strong prediction. Direct-detection experiments looking for dark matter particles will continue to find nothing regardless of how sensitive they become. The observed DM effects will continue to be explained by flow dynamics at galactic scale.

### XII.I Dark Energy as Cosmic Closure

Dark energy emerges from the universal soliton's cosmic partition. The partition closes at unity: Ω_DM + Ω_b + Ω_Λ = 1. The Ω_Λ value (approximately 0.6889) is the (251 - 22π)/264 remainder from closure — structurally determined by the other two sector fractions plus the universal-scale closure constant 251.

This is not a cosmological constant added to the universe; it is the structural remainder of the universal soliton's internal partition. The observed cosmic acceleration emerges naturally from this partition structure at cosmic scale.

### XII.J Black Holes as Drain-Limit Parent Solitons

A black hole is a parent soliton with drain capacity so large that child solitons entering its region cannot maintain their own pattern coherence. The drain overwhelms their remainder accumulation; their patterns dissolve into the parent's pattern.

The event horizon is the drain-threshold boundary — where the drain magnitude equals the critical threshold for child-soliton pattern maintenance. Inside, the drain dominates; patterns cannot persist. Outside, other channels can compete with the drain; patterns can exist.

Hawking radiation emerges from boundary channel dynamics at the drain threshold. Near the boundary, the drain hasn't fully consumed the child-pattern-maintenance capacity; quiver activity at the boundary can produce outgoing channel states that escape the drain. These outgoing states carry remainder away from the parent, reducing its drain capacity. Over long times, the black hole evaporates.

Black hole entropy (Bekenstein-Hawking formula S = A/4) emerges from counting channel configurations at the horizon's area. The specific 1/4 factor is derivable from the substrate's integer alphabet characterizing the horizon's channel-configuration density.

Information entering the black hole is absorbed into the parent soliton's pattern configuration. It is not destroyed; it is inaccessible from outside the horizon because no channel-merger can reach it. As Hawking radiation propagates outward over evaporation, the information is gradually released in the outgoing channel states.

Kerr corrections (rotating black hole differential behavior) come from the toroidal sector of the parent soliton's channel structure. Non-rotating black holes are primarily spherical; rotating black holes have additional toroidal content visible in ergosphere dynamics, frame dragging, and other effects.

### XII.K Gravitational Wave Emission

Merging solitons at the hierarchy boundary of two parent-soliton systems radiate coordinated channel activity outward. This is gravitational wave emission.

The chirp waveform (frequency sweeping upward as merger completes) comes from the channel dynamics approaching the merger-completion configuration. The emitted energy reduces the parent-soliton system's mass; the merged final state has less mass than the two separate initial states.

Gravitational wave propagation is through the quiver at c, same as photons. Both are patterns advancing one cell per tick through the substrate's baseline activity. Both produce the same cell-per-tick arithmetic identity for their propagation speeds.

## XIII. QUANTUM MECHANICS DERIVATION

### XIII.A One Dynamics

The substrate has one dynamics: channel-merger arithmetic. There is no separate "measurement" dynamics glued onto an "evolution" dynamics.

What standard QM calls "unitary evolution between measurements" is substrate channel dynamics when no observer-target merger is active. What it calls "collapse at measurement" is new channel-merger outcompeting prior channel configurations.

The measurement problem — when does collapse take over from evolution? — does not arise. The substrate has one update rule applied uniformly; there is no second dynamics to activate.

### XIII.B Observation as Entanglement

An observer is a soliton that participates in channel-merger. Observation is what happens when an observer soliton enters channel-merger with a target soliton.

Before observation: observer and target are separate solitons with their own channel configurations. Observation: their channels merge. After: they are channel-shared — one pattern with two Euclidean handles (observer location and target location).

The observer has no special status. A photographic plate, a thermometer, a detector, a conscious human — all are solitons capable of channel-merger. The distinction between "classical apparatus" and "quantum system" that the Copenhagen interpretation draws does not exist at the substrate level.

What registers an outcome is not consciousness or macroscopic scale per se; it is the extent of subsequent channel-mergers. An observer whose state shifts from the merger gets propagated into many subsequent interactions (memory, records, other observers), producing what we call "recording" the outcome. The recording is not a separate physics; it is continued channel-merger into the broader pattern network.

### XIII.C Collapse as Channel Competition

"Wavefunction collapse" does not happen as a separate physics event. What looks like collapse is new channel-merger (observer-target) outcompeting and displacing prior channel configurations (the system's isolated coherence).

If the system was in a superposition of channel states, and an observer merges with it, the strongest channel-merger wins. The observer-target pattern resolves to the specific configuration determined by the merger dynamics. The observer registers the outcome corresponding to that configuration.

From the observer's perspective, this looks like collapse: "the state was uncertain, now it's definite." From the substrate's perspective, it's channel-merger competition: stronger merger displaces weaker ones.

### XIII.D Decoherence as Environmental Merger

Decoherence — the loss of quantum coherence from environmental interaction — is continuous channel-merger with environmental solitons.

A prepared quantum system in isolation has few channel interactions beyond its internal structure. Over time, environmental channels (thermal noise, background radiation, incidental particle interactions) establish channel-merger with the system. As environmental mergers accumulate, they outcompete the system's isolated coherence; the system's effective quantum state is dominated by environmental-merger averaging.

This is why quantum coherence is fragile. Any soliton has many environmental channels; without extensive isolation (cryogenic, vacuum, shielded), environmental channel-merger dominates.

### XIII.E Channel-Sharing Entanglement

Entangled solitons share channel substrate. They are not separate solitons linked by an edge; they are one pattern with multiple Euclidean handles.

The endpoints appear in Euclidean projection to be separate particles at some distance apart. In the substrate graph, they are graph-adjacent through the shared channel. The "distance" between them in 3D space is a projection artifact of how the graph folds into observable space.

When one endpoint is measured, the channel-shared pattern resolves to a specific configuration. Because the endpoints share one channel, the outcome at each endpoint is the correlated outcome of the shared configuration. This is Bell correlation, not signal transmission.

### XIII.F Bell Non-Locality Dissolved

Bell's theorem proves that local hidden variable theories cannot reproduce observed correlations at arbitrary distances. The substrate is not a local hidden variable theory; it is local in the graph sense (channel-sharing is graph-adjacent) and non-local only in the Euclidean projection.

The apparent non-locality in Euclidean coordinates is the graph-adjacency being projected across arbitrary Euclidean distance. Nothing superluminal is happening; the channel-sharing connects graph-adjacent endpoints at one tick, but the endpoints' Euclidean projections can be arbitrarily far apart.

### XIII.G No-Signaling as Channel Symmetry

The no-signaling theorem (local measurements on A produce no observable change in B's marginal statistics) emerges from the symmetry of the channel-sharing. The channel connects endpoints symmetrically; there is no sender-receiver asymmetry.

Measurement at endpoint A resolves the shared pattern; B's endpoint is now in the corresponding state. But no directional information is transmitted — both endpoints are resolved simultaneously because they share one pattern, not because anything traveled between them.

This structural symmetry prevents faster-than-light signaling while preserving the correlations. Standard physics derives no-signaling from the mathematical structure of quantum mechanics; the substrate derives it from the graph structure of channel-sharing.

### XIII.H Monogamy of Entanglement

An entanglement channel connects specific solitons. When soliton A shares a channel with soliton B, additional channel-merger with soliton C must either split the existing channel (weakening A-B entanglement) or create new channels (not a direct A-B-C three-way entanglement in simple channel-sharing).

This is the combinatorial source of entanglement monogamy. A particle cannot be simultaneously maximally entangled with two others because one channel has specific endpoints, and additional merger with a third endpoint dilutes or changes the structure.

### XIII.I Multi-Particle Entanglement (GHZ, W, Cluster States)

Multi-particle entangled states correspond to coordinated channel-sharing patterns with multiple endpoints.

**GHZ state** (|000⟩ + |111⟩)/√2: three endpoints sharing a coordinated channel with specific parity structure.

**W state** ((|100⟩ + |010⟩ + |001⟩))/√3: three endpoints sharing a channel with different internal structure.

**Cluster state:** four or more endpoints sharing a channel whose internal structure matches the cluster graph topology.

These are not hypergraph edges (single n-ary connections); they are coordinated channel-sharings, with the coordination structure matching the multi-particle state's symmetry. This resolves the pairwise-vs-hypergraph question in the PCTRM-2 errata: channels are not being "wired between separate solitons"; the multi-particle entangled state is one pattern with multiple Euclidean handles, and the coordination is intrinsic to the pattern.

### XIII.J Single-Particle Interference

Single-particle interference (double-slit) emerges from channel-agreement dynamics at termination events.

Without observer at the slits: the photon's channel structure has multiple candidate paths (through slit A, through slit B). Channel-agreement resolves all available paths simultaneously — contributions from both slits accumulate in the channel structure. At termination (absorption at screen), the full accumulated structure determines the probability of absorption at each screen position. The interference pattern is this accumulation's spatial structure.

With observer at slits: the observer enters the channel-agreement candidate pool. Agreement can resolve with the observer absorbing the photon at one slit. If it does, the photon terminates there; the downstream pattern is single-path particle behavior. If it doesn't, the photon continues, but the observer's presence in the pool has modified the available resolutions.

Nothing "chose" a path mid-flight. The channel structure propagated through all candidates; termination resolved based on the full configuration at the termination event.

### XIII.K Quantum Eraser

Delayed-choice eraser experiments demonstrate that the interference pattern can be restored or eliminated based on decisions made after the photon has reached the detector — but before the data is analyzed.

In the substrate picture: termination events determine the channel resolution. The full channel context at termination (including entanglement structures with which-path markers) determines whether the termination appears as interference (full channel context preserved) or as particle behavior (which-path marker has been absorbed, reducing the available paths).

There is no retrocausality. What happens between emission and termination is irrelevant to the observable outcome; only the full termination-event context matters. The appearance of retrocausality comes from thinking the photon had a definite mid-flight path that subsequent choices "changed"; in the substrate picture, the photon had no mid-flight path — its channel structure was accumulating until termination.

### XIII.L Born Rule Derivation

The Born rule — probability of outcome α is |⟨α|ψ⟩|² — is derivable from substrate structure:

**Step 1:** The substrate is a unit-adjacency graph. All channel-direction states are unit vectors on the appropriate sphere.

**Step 2:** Measurement is channel-merger at termination; the channel's state is resolved by projection onto the measurement basis.

**Step 3:** Basis-independent positive-real content extraction from a unit-vector state requires round-trip closure: forward projection ⟨α|ψ⟩ plus conjugate closure ⟨ψ|α⟩ = |⟨α|ψ⟩|². One-way projection gives complex amplitudes (signed); round-trip gives positive reals summing to unity over a complete basis.

**Step 4:** The exponent 2 in squared magnitude counts two conversions (forward + conjugate). This matches MATH-11's β² pattern (one angular integration = β²) and PHYS-49's ppm-to-ppb verified exponent counting at QED four-loop precision.

The Born rule is not axiomatic; it is the structural consequence of round-trip measurement on unit-adjacency graphs.

### XIII.M Hilbert Space as Continuous Limit

Hilbert space is the continuous-limit description of a unit-adjacency graph with complex-valued channel states. The unit-sphere structure, inner product, Hermitian operator structure — all emerge from graph adjacency.

Standard QM's state space is PCTRM's state space at coarse-grained resolution. When we zoom out from individual cells and ticks to macroscopic observations, the discrete graph becomes a continuous manifold of unit vectors with inner products measuring graph-edge relationships. Hilbert space is what that looks like.

Complex amplitudes emerge from the two-sector channel state structure. The spherical sector contributes magnitude (unit direction); the toroidal sector contributes phase (elliptic period). The complex representation combines these naturally.

### XIII.N Wave-Particle Duality Dissolved

The wave-particle duality is not a fundamental property of quantum objects; it is different manifestations of channel-agreement dynamics.

Wave pattern: channel-agreement across multiple paths without observer interruption. Multiple paths contribute to the termination event, producing interference.

Particle pattern: channel-agreement resolved to single path (by observer in candidate pool). One path dominates termination, producing particle-like behavior.

The substrate's channel-agreement dynamics produces both. Nothing is "both wave and particle." The two descriptions are the two modes of channel-agreement resolution.

## XIV. STANDARD MODEL REDUCTION

### XIV.A Parallel Isomorphism

PCTRM and the Standard Model produce identical observables from different primitives. This is the parallel-isomorphism commitment: wherever both frameworks make a prediction, they produce the same numerical value at measurement precision.

The framework does not claim to make different predictions than SM; it claims to produce the same predictions through substrate mechanism rather than postulation.

### XIV.B Fermion Generation Count = 3

There are three generations of fermions (e, μ, τ; u/d, c/s, t/b). This is a structural fact of the substrate's channel enumeration. The substrate supports exactly three generations.

The specific mechanism (why 3 rather than 2 or 4) is pending full specification. The generations correspond to three channel-configuration options in the substrate's subatomic structure.

### XIV.C Generation Democracy

The three generations have identical structural roles in the gauge group. The lepton generation democracy (db₁ = db₂ = db₃ = 4/3) is a substrate channel-closure identity. All three generations produce the same Fraction(4, 3) for their Cabibbo-doublet sum, reflecting structural symmetry in their channel organization.

### XIV.D Gauge Group Structure

The Standard Model gauge group U(1) × SU(2) × SU(3) arises from substrate channel type enumeration.

U(1): electromagnetic channel (single-type, always-active between charged solitons).

SU(2): weak channel structure with two-component internal symmetry.

SU(3): strong channel structure with three-component internal symmetry (color).

The specific mapping from substrate channel structure to these gauge groups is pending full specification. The channels at different hierarchy levels have specific internal structure that corresponds to these group dimensions.

### XIV.E Koide K = 2/3

The lepton Koide formula K = (m_e + m_μ + m_τ) / (√m_e + √m_μ + √m_τ)² = 2/3 at 9.2 ppm precision.

This reflects the substrate's lepton channel closure structure. The three lepton generations' channel patterns close in a specific geometric sense that produces K = 2/3 as an exact structural identity.

### XIV.F CKM Matrix Structure

The CKM matrix elements are integer channel ratios:

V_us = 9/40 (at 44 ppm against PDG reference)

V_cb = 1/24 (at 0.37%)

The gap ratio 38/27 from Cabibbo doublet channel arithmetic.

These are substrate-level structural identities, not measured parameters. The values emerge from counting channel configurations for flavor-changing transitions.

### XIV.G Anomalous Magnetic Moments

The anomalous magnetic moments a_e and a_μ are computed from the three-layer decomposition:

Spherical modulus: β² content, dominant for the electron.

Number-theoretic Layer 1: ζ, Li, rational content.

Toroidal-geometric Layer 2: K(k), E(k) content at topology-specific moduli, dominant for the muon.

PHYS-49 has validated this decomposition at QED four-loop precision with sub-ppm extracted moduli (k₈₁ = 0.999994, k₈₃ = 0.997130). The decomposition produces measured a_e and a_μ values at Harvard and FNAL precision.

### XIV.H Renormalization Group Running

Coupling constants run with energy scale in standard QFT. In the substrate picture, this running is the scale-dependent averaging of channel configurations.

At high energy (short wavelength), probes resolve finer substrate structure; couplings reflect fine-scale channel activity.

At low energy (long wavelength), probes see coarse-grained substrate; couplings reflect averaged channel activity.

The running equations emerge from this scale-dependent averaging; they are not postulated.

### XIV.I QCD Confinement

Quark confinement (no free quarks observed at normal energies) comes from the toroidal gluon flux tubes binding quarks within Level 3 nucleon solitons.

The flux tubes are toroidal channel patterns — the substrate's toroidal-sector manifestation at nuclear scale. They carry 99% of nucleon mass as Higgs-tick-cost for maintaining the flux tube pattern.

Asymptotic freedom (weak coupling at high energy) comes from toroidal flux tubes becoming smaller relative to shorter-wavelength probes — at high energy, probes can penetrate the flux tube structure and see nearly free quarks.

### XIV.J Neutrino Oscillation and CP Violation

Neutrino oscillation emerges from weak-channel arithmetic with flavor-mixing. The PMNS matrix elements are substrate-level structural identities analogous to CKM.

CP violation (observed in weak interactions) comes from channel-state symmetry breaking in weak channel structure. The specific CP-violating phase and its magnitude are substrate-level quantities that need alphabet expression.

### XIV.K Higgs Mechanism

The Higgs mechanism is the substrate's mass-generation mechanism. Massive particles couple to the Higgs pattern (a specific substrate configuration) that imposes per-tick cost on their direction updates.

The Higgs boson itself (at 125 GeV mass) is the excitation of the Higgs pattern — a soliton that emerges when the Higgs substrate configuration oscillates. Its mass is its own Higgs-tick-cost for pattern maintenance.

Electroweak symmetry breaking at the Higgs scale is the substrate transition point where Higgs coupling becomes operational for mass generation. Below this scale, Higgs-mass-generation is active; above, the symmetry is unbroken.

### XIV.L Mass Hierarchy from Per-Boundary Modulus

The observed mass hierarchy (m_e ≈ 0.511 MeV, m_μ ≈ 106 MeV, m_τ ≈ 1777 MeV for leptons; similar hierarchical spread for quarks) emerges from the per-hierarchy-boundary modulus structure.

Each hierarchy boundary has its own modulus. The masses of particles at different hierarchy positions are alphabet expressions involving these boundary moduli. The specific expressions produce the observed mass values at CODATA precision.

### XIV.M Weak Interaction Specifics

Weak channels carry specific structure for flavor-changing interactions. The W and Z bosons are massive solitons mediating weak channel dynamics. Their mass (W ~ 80 GeV, Z ~ 91 GeV) is their Higgs-tick-cost; their short range (~10⁻¹⁸ m) is the distance their massive-carrier pattern can propagate before the Higgs-cost exhausts their remainder.

Weak-channel conservation laws (conservation of flavor in strong/EM interactions, non-conservation in weak interactions) reflect which substrate-level interactions can change flavor configurations.
